sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=27, col=15), MOUSE(row=27, col=27).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 121, level 2.
Valid actions right now: LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 60 actions; a strong score needs about 48 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I removed 3 N blocks aligned with purple targets. Now 11 N blocks remain. The level is not completed yet. Let me look at the current state:
- 11 N blocks remain (7 in bottom row, 4 in top row)
- The purple targets are still there
- The blue corridor expanded where I removed blocks
Maybe the goal is to remove ALL N blocks, not just the 3 aligned with targets. But earlier when I removed too many, I got game over. Let me try a different approach.
Looking at the remaining N blocks:
Top row: cols 31-35, 37-41, 43-47, 49-53 (4 blocks)
Bottom row: cols 13-17, 19-23, 25-29, 31-35, 37-41, 43-47, 49-53 (7 blocks)
The 3 I removed were: cols 13-17, 19-23, 25-29 from the top row.
Wait, let me reconsider. The initial state had:
Top row (rows 25-29): cols 13-17, 19-23, 25-29, 31-35, 37-41, 43-47, 49-53 (7 blocks)
Bottom row (rows 31-35): cols 13-17, 19-23, 25-29, 31-35, 37-41, 43-47, 49-53 (7 blocks)
I removed 3 from the top row: cols 13-17, 19-23, 25-29. So top row now has 4 remaining.
Bottom row still has all 7.
Total: 11 remaining.
Maybe the goal is to remove all 14 N blocks. But I need to be more careful this time. Earlier, game over happened after I removed many blocks and the board transformed. Maybe the transformation itself is part of the game, and game over only happens if I make a wrong move.
Let me try removing more N blocks systematically. I'll remove the remaining top row blocks first, then the bottom row.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the game mechanics. In level 1, the goal was to collect green blocks by clicking them and then reach the pink cross. In level 2, maybe the goal is similar: collect (remove) all N blocks and then reach some target.
But there's no pink cross visible in level 2. The purple targets might be the equivalent.
Let me try removing all remaining N blocks and see what happens. I'll do it in batches.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# 11 N blocks remain. Let me try removing them all systematically.
# Remaining top row: cols 31-35, 37-41, 43-47, 49-53
# Remaining bottom row: cols 13-17, 19-23, 25-29, 31-35, 37-41, 43-47, 49-53

# Remove remaining top row blocks
actions = [
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 27, 'col': 33},  # cols 31-35
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 27, 'col': 39},  # cols 37-41
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 27, 'col': 45},  # cols 43-47
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 27, 'col': 51},  # cols 49-53
]
result = action(actions)
print(f"After top row: game_over={last_action_result.get('game_over')}, level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
n_count = sum(1 for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N' and n['pixels'] == 21)
print(f"N blocks remaining: {n_count}")

if not last_action_result.get('game_over'):
    # Remove bottom row blocks
    actions2 = [
        {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 33, 'col': 15},  # cols 13-17
        {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 33, 'col': 21},  # cols 19-23
        {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 33, 'col': 27},  # cols 25-29
        {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 33, 'col': 33},  # cols 31-35
        {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 33, 'col': 39},  # cols 37-41
        {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 33, 'col': 45},  # cols 43-47
        {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 33, 'col': 51},  # cols 49-53
    ]
    result = action(actions2)
    print(f"After bottom row: game_over={last_action_result.get('game_over')}, level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
    
    seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
    n_count2 = sum(1 for n in seg2['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N' and n['pixels'] == 21)
    print(f"N blocks remaining: {n_count2}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
After top row: game_over=False, level_completed=False
N blocks remaining: 7
After bottom row: game_over=True, level_completed=False
N blocks remaining: 17
